senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of resit: to take an exam again, usually because you failed or did not do well the first time.
examples: She resat the maths exam in June and passed with a higher grade.
Students who failed the test resat it at the end of the term.

senses.definition: The act of satisfying someone or something again.
examples: The company offered a full refund as a form of resatisfaction for unhappy customers.
True resatisfaction was only possible after they corrected all the mistakes.

senses.definition: To satisfy someone or something again.
examples: The updated policy was designed to resatisfy long‑time customers.
They worked hard to resatisfy the strict safety requirements.

senses.definition: To save a file, document, or data again, especially after making changes.
examples: Remember to resave your document after you edit it.
You should resave the image in a different format.

senses.definition: To saw wood again, especially to cut a piece of timber into thinner boards.
examples: The carpenter decided to resaw the plank into two thinner boards.
You can resaw the lumber to get the exact thickness you need.

senses.definition: A machine or process used for sawing wood again, usually to make thinner boards from thicker timber.
examples: The workshop invested in a new resaw for processing large beams.
Using a resaw can greatly reduce waste when cutting lumber.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of resaw: sawed wood again, especially to make it thinner.
examples: They resawed the old beams into new floorboards.
The timber was resawed to create matching panels.

senses.definition: A person or machine that resaws wood.
examples: The resawer adjusted the settings before cutting the beam.
This industrial resawer can process very large logs.

senses.definition: The act of sawing wood again, especially to split it into thinner pieces.
examples: Resawing thick lumber can save money on materials.
He spent the afternoon resawing boards for the project.

senses.definition: Past participle of resaw: wood that has been sawn again, usually into thinner pieces.
examples: The resawn boards were stacked to dry.
Resawn timber often shows a more interesting grain pattern.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resaw: saws wood again, especially to make it thinner.
examples: The mill resaws thick planks into thin boards.
He resaws old beams to create new flooring.

senses.definition: Plural of resaw: machines used for sawing wood again into thinner pieces.
examples: The factory operates several resaws for different types of lumber.
Modern resaws can handle extremely wide boards.

senses.definition: A worker who operates a resaw machine.
examples: The resawyer must be trained to handle large logs safely.
As a resawyer, he is responsible for maintaining the equipment.

senses.definition: To say something again, often in a different way.
examples: Could you resay that more simply, please?
He paused to resay his point so that everyone could understand.

senses.definition: The act of saying something again, often to make it clearer.
examples: Resaying the instructions slowly helped the children follow them.
She kept resaying her apology, hoping he would forgive her.

senses.definition: Third person singular of resay: says something again, often in a different way.
examples: When he is nervous, he resays the same phrase over and over.
She resays her main argument at the end of the speech.

senses.definition: A blue chemical dye used as an indicator to test for the presence of living cells or bacteria.
examples: Resazurin is often used in cell‑viability assays.
The solution changes color when resazurin is reduced by active bacteria.

senses.definition: To change the scale of something, especially a measurement, image, or graph, so that it is larger, smaller, or uses different units.
examples: You may need to rescale the image so it fits on the page.
The data were rescaled before the analysis.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rescale: changed the scale of something.
examples: The chart was rescaled to make the differences clearer.
They rescaled the photo to reduce the file size.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rescale: changes the scale of something.
examples: The program automatically rescales the window.
This function rescales the data to a common range.

senses.definition: The act of changing the scale of something, such as an image, graph, or set of numbers.
examples: Rescaling the picture can improve how it looks on the screen.
The model required rescaling of all input variables.

senses.definition: To scan something again, such as a document, barcode, or body part using a machine.
examples: The image was blurry, so they had to rescan the page.
The doctor decided to rescan the patient’s knee.

senses.definition: An act of scanning something again.
examples: The doctor ordered a rescan to confirm the result.
A rescan of the hard disk showed no errors.

senses.definition: The process of particles or waves being scattered again after an initial scattering.
examples: The model takes rescattering effects into account.
Multiple rescattering of electrons can change the final image.

senses.definition: To change the time or date of an event to a new one.
examples: We need to reschedule the meeting for next week.
The flight was rescheduled due to bad weather.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of reschedule: changed the time or date of an event.
examples: The concert was rescheduled for July.
They rescheduled the interview at my request.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reschedule: changes the time or date of something.
examples: If it rains, the organizer reschedules the games.
The doctor often reschedules appointments when there is an emergency.

senses.definition: The act of changing the time or date of something.
examples: Rescheduling the event caused some confusion.
We are rescheduling all the classes for next month.

senses.definition: Plural of rescheduling: several acts of changing times or dates.
examples: After many reschedulings, the project finally started.
Frequent reschedulings can frustrate participants.

senses.definition: To send someone to school again or to provide them with new or different education.
examples: The program aims to reschool adults who left education early.
They decided to reschool their child at a different institution.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of reschool: gave someone new schooling or sent them to school again.
examples: Many unemployed people were reschooled for new careers.
Their daughter was reschooled at a specialist academy.

senses.definition: The process of giving people new schooling or education, especially to help them change careers or improve skills.
examples: Reschooling can help older workers adapt to a changing job market.
The government funded reschooling programs for former miners.

senses.definition: Third person singular of reschool: gives someone new schooling or sends them to a different school.
examples: The charity reschools young people who dropped out.
The government reschools prisoners to prepare them for release.

senses.definition: To officially cancel a law, decision, agreement, or offer so that it is no longer in effect.
examples: The company decided to rescind the job offer.
The government may rescind the new regulation.

senses.definition: That can be rescinded; able to be officially cancelled.
examples: The agreement is rescindable within fourteen days.
They insisted on a rescindable clause in the contract.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rescind: officially cancelled something such as a law or agreement.
examples: The invitation was rescinded without explanation.
They rescinded the policy after many complaints.

senses.definition: A person or authority that rescinds a law, agreement, or decision.
examples: The court acts as the rescinder of unlawful regulations.
In this case, the rescinder of the contract was the buyer.

senses.definition: Plural of rescinder: people or authorities that rescind laws, agreements, or decisions.
examples: The courts are the ultimate rescinders of unjust laws.
Different agencies may act as rescinders in different situations.

senses.definition: The act of officially cancelling a law, decision, or agreement.
examples: Rescinding the law took several months.
They are considering rescinding the previous offer.

senses.definition: The act or process of rescinding; rescission.
examples: The rescindment of the agreement must be in writing.
There are strict rules governing the rescindment of such contracts.

senses.definition: Plural of rescindment: several acts or instances of rescinding.
examples: Repeated rescindments of policies created uncertainty.
The report listed all rescindments made during the year.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rescind: officially cancels a law, decision, or agreement.
examples: The board rescinds any rule that is no longer needed.
If the buyer fails to pay, the seller rescinds the contract.

senses.definition: Able to be rescinded; open to cancellation by law.
examples: The court found the agreement rescissible because of fraud.
A rescissible contract can be set aside under certain conditions.

senses.definition: The act of officially ending a law, contract, or agreement so that it is no longer valid.
examples: The court ordered the rescission of the contract.
Rescission of the policy left many people without coverage.

senses.definition: Plural of rescission: several acts of cancelling laws, contracts, or agreements.
examples: The report details all rescissions made during the fiscal year.
Frequent rescissions of policies can damage trust.

senses.definition: Relating to rescission; having the power or effect of cancelling something by law.
examples: The court issued a rescissory decree.
Rescissory actions are taken when a contract is voidable.

senses.definition: To give a new score to something, such as a test, game, or credit file.
examples: The teacher agreed to rescore the exam.
The agency can rescore your credit if there is new information.

senses.definition: To write new music for a film, show, or performance.
examples: They decided to rescore the movie for the new release.
The composer was hired to rescore several scenes.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rescore: gave something a new score.
examples: The exam was rescored after a mistake was found.
They rescored the game when the referee changed his decision.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rescore in the musical sense: wrote new music for something.
examples: The classic film was completely rescored for its anniversary edition.
They rescored the advertisement to match the new brand image.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rescore: gives something a new score.
examples: The computer rescores the test automatically.
The lender rescores your credit when new data arrives.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rescore in the musical sense: writes new music for something.
examples: The studio often rescores old films for modern audiences.
He rescores commercials to give them a fresh sound.

senses.definition: The act of giving something a new score.
examples: Rescoring exams can be time‑consuming.
They are rescoring all applications under the new system.

senses.definition: The act of writing new music for a film, show, or performance.
examples: Rescoring the film changed its mood completely.
The project involved rescoring several classic cartoons.

senses.definition: A person or device that counts again; one that recounts.
examples: An independent rescounter checked the votes.
The machine served as a fast rescounter of the coins.

senses.definition: An old legal term meaning the unlawful rescue of a person or property from legal custody.
examples: In older cases, the defendant was charged with rescous.
Rescous was treated as an offence against the authority of the court.

senses.definition: To scramble something again, such as signals, data, or the order of items.
examples: The system will rescramble the signal every few minutes.
If you do not like the arrangement, you can rescramble the list.
